meaning: A popular file format and software used for file compression and decompression
example: The server uses gzip to compress the website's code so it loads faster.
example2: You can recognize compressed files by the '.gz' extension.
example3: Gzip is very efficient for compressing text-based files like HTML and CSS.
Explanation: Gzip (GNU Zip) is the 'gold standard' for compression on the internet. When you visit a website, the server often 'gzips' the data before sending it to your browser. This can reduce the file size by 70% or more, making the internet feel much faster. It uses the 'DEFLATE' algorithm, which looks for repeating patterns in text. It is a 'Lossless' compression, meaning the file is exactly the same after you decompress it. It is widely used in Linux environments and is a standard part of web server software like Apache and Nginx.
usageTip: Always enable gzip on your web server to improve your site's SEO and speed.
commonMistake: Trying to gzip images like JPEG; images are already compressed and gzipping them can actually make them bigger.

meaning: A software application and file format used for compressing files to save space
example: Most websites use Gzip to compress their files before sending them to your browser.
example2: You can Gzip a large log file to reduce its size from 100MB to 10MB.
example3: The Gzip format is very popular because it is fast and free.
Explanation: Gzip (GNU zip) is the standard tool for compressing data on the web and in Linux systems. When you visit a website, the server often 'Gzips' the HTML and CSS files, making them much smaller so they load faster on your phone. The file extension is usually '.gz'. It is a 'lossless' compression, meaning no data is lost during the process. It is widely used by developers to speed up websites and save storage space on servers.
usageTip: Enable Gzip compression on your web server to improve page load speed.
commonMistake: Confusing Gzip with 'Zip' (they are different formats, though they do the same job).
